intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: Users find gradual, animated state changes more engaging and less jarring than instant updates when tracking health metrics over time
facade.label: Smooth transitions
facade.acceptImplies: The dashboard should use fade-ins, slide animations, and eased transitions when data updates, metric values change, or users navigate between sections
facade.rejectImplies: The dashboard should prioritize instant, snappy updates with no animation—data appears immediately, page changes are cut-to-black, interactions have zero latency feel

evidence.hypothesis: Users find gradual, animated state changes more engaging and less jarring than instant updates when tracking health metrics over time
evidence.implication: The dashboard should use fade-ins, slide animations, and eased transitions when data updates, metric values change, or users navigate between sections

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer scannable visual chunks (cards showing one metric each) over dense statistical tables for tracking experiments
facade.label: Daily metrics cards
facade.acceptImplies: Design favors modular, card-based layouts where each health metric (weight, sleep, mood) gets its own contained unit with one number prominent
facade.rejectImplies: Design favors consolidated views — either a single unified number (aggregate score) or a full data table showing all metrics at once for comparison

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer soft, ambient visual feedback over stark data displays when tracking health experiments
facade.axisTargeted: Visual warmth vs clinical coldness in data presentation
facade.label: Warm glow
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard should use warm color palettes, soft shadows, gentle transitions, and forgiving typography to create a sense of care and encouragement rather than sterile metrics
facade.rejectImplies: Dashboard should prioritize clarity, contrast, and clinical precision with cooler tones and direct data hierarchy to feel scientifically rigorous and trustworthy

facade.hypothesis: A health dashboard feels less clinical and more approachable when it uses diffused lighting and rounded containers instead of sharp lines and flat surfaces.
facade.label: Soft shadows, gentle curves
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers organic, calming aesthetics over clinical precision. Likely drawn to cards with border-radius, subtle depth, muted colors.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers clarity and efficiency over visual softness. May prefer flat design, high contrast, sharp edges, minimal shadow effects.

facade.hypothesis: Users tracking health experiments prefer calming, organic color palettes over bright clinical interfaces
facade.label: Warm muted tones
facade.acceptImplies: The dashboard should use earthy, desaturated colors (taupes, soft greens, warm grays) to feel personal and reflective rather than clinical
facade.rejectImplies: Users may prefer cooler, crisper aesthetics or brighter accents to feel modern and energized during health tracking

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer warm, conversational language for health tracking over clinical terminology
facade.label: Friendly check-ins
facade.acceptImplies: Language should feel like a supportive friend asking about your day, not a medical form. Warmth builds habit.
facade.rejectImplies: Users want neutral, professional language—clinical tone feels more trustworthy for health data. Personality feels frivolous.

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer immediate, snappy number updates without animation when viewing health metrics, as delays feel sluggish when they're seeking quick data confirmation
facade.label: Instant number pop
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard metric values should update instantly or near-instantly with minimal transition time; users value speed of data confirmation over smoothness
facade.rejectImplies: Instant updates feel cold or disconnected; users want perceivable motion to help them notice and process changes in their health data
